charts.title: Week 1 Topic Map
charts.description: High-level overview of all major topics covered in Week 1: Non-Cooperative Games.

nodes.label:
Week 1
Non-Cooperative Games
nodes.note: Week 1 introduces game theory as a framework for modelling strategic interactions between rational, self-interested agents. Core topics span normal form games, Nash equilibria, dominance reasoning, and mixed strategies.

nodes.label:
Intro: MAS
& Game Theory
nodes.note: Multi-agent systems can be cooperative or competitive. The competitive case is modelled as a non-cooperative game, studied through Algorithmic Game Theory and Computational Social Choice.

nodes.label:
Normal
Form Games
nodes.note: A normal (strategic) form game is defined by a tuple of players, strategy sets, and utility functions. It is the canonical representation for simultaneous-move games.

nodes.label:
Classical
Games
nodes.note: Classical examples such as the Prisoner's Dilemma, Matching Pennies, and Battle of the Sexes illustrate key game-theoretic phenomena including social dilemmas, zero-sum conflict, and coordination problems.

nodes.label: Dominance
nodes.note: A strategy is dominant if it is always at least as good as any alternative, regardless of opponents' play. Iterated elimination of dominated strategies (IEDS) can reduce or solve games without computing equilibria directly.

nodes.label:
Pure Strategy
Nash Equilibrium
nodes.note: A Pure Strategy Nash Equilibrium is a strategy profile where no player can improve their payoff by unilaterally switching strategies. It formalises the idea of a stable, self-enforcing outcome.

nodes.label:
Mixed Strategy
Nash Equilibrium
nodes.note: A Mixed Strategy NE extends Nash equilibrium to probability distributions over pure strategies. Nash (1950) proved every finite game has at least one NE in mixed strategies.

nodes.label:
Maximin &
Minimax
nodes.note: Maximin strategies maximise a player's worst-case payoff, providing a safety guarantee. In two-player zero-sum games the minimax theorem guarantees maximin equals minimax, and every maximin strategy is a Nash equilibrium.

nodes.label:
Other Solution
Concepts
nodes.note: Beyond Nash equilibrium, solution concepts include rationalizable strategies (consistent with common knowledge of rationality), correlated equilibrium (coordination via a mediator), and strong equilibrium (coalition-proof stability).

nodes.label:
Algorithmic
Game Theory
nodes.note: AGT combines theoretical CS and economics to study equilibrium computation, mechanism design, and the algorithmic tractability of game-theoretic problems.

nodes.label:
Computational
Social Choice
nodes.note: COMSOC studies collective decision-making and fair division, asking how group preferences can be aggregated efficiently and fairly using computational methods.

nodes.label:
Multi-Agent
Systems
nodes.note: A MAS consists of multiple interacting agents. Competitive agents optimise individual utility with no binding contracts, making non-cooperative game theory the natural model.

nodes.label:
Definition &
Components
nodes.note: A game is a tuple (N, S_i, U_i): a set of players N, a strategy set S_i per player, and a utility function U_i mapping strategy profiles to real-valued payoffs.

nodes.label:
Strategy Profiles
& Notation
nodes.note: A strategy profile s = (s_1, ..., s_n) specifies every player's choice. The shorthand s_{-i} denotes all strategies except player i's, allowing compact statements of best-response conditions.

nodes.label:
Prisoner's
Dilemma
nodes.note: The Prisoner's Dilemma is the canonical social dilemma: individual rationality (Defect is dominant) leads both players to (D,D), a Pareto-inferior outcome compared to mutual cooperation (C,C).

nodes.label:
Coordination
& Battle of Sexes
nodes.note: Coordination games have multiple pure Nash equilibria. Battle of the Sexes additionally introduces conflicting preferences: both players want to coordinate but prefer different joint outcomes.

nodes.label:
Zero-Sum
Games
nodes.note: In zero-sum games, one player's gain is exactly the other's loss. The minimax theorem guarantees that both players' maximin values equal the game's unique value, and any maximin strategy pair is a Nash equilibrium.

nodes.label:
Strict
Dominance
nodes.note: Strategy s strictly dominates t if s gives strictly higher payoff than t for every opponent strategy profile. Rational players never play strictly dominated strategies.

nodes.label:
Weak
Dominance
nodes.note: Strategy s weakly dominates t if s is at least as good as t in all cases and strictly better in at least one. Eliminating weakly dominated strategies may depend on the order of elimination.

nodes.label: IEDS
nodes.note: Iterated Elimination of Dominated Strategies repeatedly removes dominated strategies. For strict dominance the result is order-independent; for weak dominance the order can matter.

nodes.label: Best Response
nodes.note: A best response for player i is a strategy that maximises i's payoff given the strategies of all other players. A Nash equilibrium is precisely a profile where every player plays a best response.

nodes.label: Properties
nodes.note: PSNE need not be unique (coordination game has two) and may not exist (Matching Pennies has none). Every dominant strategy equilibrium is a Nash equilibrium but not vice versa.

nodes.label:
Mixed Strategies
& Expected Utility
nodes.note: A mixed strategy is a probability distribution over pure strategies. Expected utility under a mixed profile is the probability-weighted average of pure-strategy payoffs.

nodes.label:
Indifference
Principle
nodes.note: In an MSNE, each player randomises only over strategies that give equal expected utility. Crucially, player i's mixing probabilities are determined by making the other player indifferent, not by maximising i's own expected payoff.

nodes.label:
Nash's
Theorem 1950
nodes.note: Nash proved in 1950 that every finite game (finitely many players and actions) has at least one Nash equilibrium in mixed strategies, guaranteeing equilibrium existence even when no pure NE exists.

nodes.label:
Maximin
Strategy
nodes.note: The maximin strategy maximises the minimum payoff a player can guarantee, assuming opponents play adversarially. Mixed strategies generally yield higher safety-level values than pure strategies.

nodes.label:
Minimax
Strategy
nodes.note: The minimax strategy for player i against j is the strategy that minimises j's best possible payoff, useful for punishment in repeated games and for computing the minimax value.

nodes.label:
Minimax
Theorem
nodes.note: Von Neumann's minimax theorem states that in any finite two-player zero-sum game, the maximin value equals the minimax value. All Nash equilibria share the same payoff vector and every maximin/minimax strategy pair is a NE.

nodes.label:
Rationalizable
Strategies
nodes.note: A strategy is rationalizable if it is a best response to some belief about opponents that is itself consistent with common knowledge of rationality. All Nash equilibrium strategies are rationalizable, but not all rationalizable strategies are Nash.

nodes.label:
Correlated
Equilibrium
nodes.note: A correlated equilibrium allows players to condition actions on a shared random signal from a mediator. The set of correlated equilibria is a superset of Nash equilibria and can always be computed via linear programming.

nodes.label:
Strong
Equilibrium
nodes.note: A strong equilibrium (Aumann) requires no coalition of any size to have a jointly improving deviation. It is strictly stronger than Nash equilibrium and rarely exists; the Prisoner's Dilemma has no strong equilibrium.

charts.title: Iterated Elimination of Dominated Strategies
charts.description: Step-by-step procedure for solving a game via IEDS.

nodes.label: Start: normal form game
nodes.note: We begin with a game in normal form — a payoff matrix listing every player's utility for each combination of strategies.

nodes.label:
Find strictly dominated
strategy for any player
nodes.note: For each player, check whether any strategy is strictly worse than some other strategy (or mixture) for every opponent strategy combination.

nodes.label:
Dominated strategy
found?
nodes.note: If a strictly dominated strategy exists for any player, it can be safely removed — a rational player would never choose it. If none remain, the process halts.

nodes.label:
Eliminate strategy
from game
nodes.note: Remove the dominated strategy from the matrix, producing a smaller game. For strict dominance, the order of elimination does not affect the final result.

nodes.label:
Single strategy
per player?
nodes.note: If only one strategy remains for each player, IEDS has fully solved the game and identified a unique Nash equilibrium. Otherwise, the surviving strategies form a restricted game.

nodes.label:
SOLVED
Unique Nash equilibrium
nodes.note: IEDS has eliminated all but one strategy per player. The surviving profile is the unique Nash equilibrium of the game.

nodes.label:
Partial solution
Surviving strategies remain
nodes.note: No further dominated strategies exist but multiple strategies survive. The game is only partially solved — further analysis (e.g. best-response enumeration) is needed to find Nash equilibria.

charts.title: Finding Pure Strategy Nash Equilibria
charts.description: Best response method for identifying all pure strategy Nash equilibria in a normal form game.

nodes.label: Start: payoff matrix
nodes.note: Begin with a normal form game payoff matrix. We will find all pure strategy Nash equilibria by marking each player's best responses.

nodes.label:
For each column, mark
row player best response
nodes.note: Fix each column (opponent's strategy) in turn and identify which row gives the row player the highest payoff. Mark these cells.

nodes.label:
For each row, mark
col player best response
nodes.note: Fix each row (opponent's strategy) in turn and identify which column gives the column player the highest payoff. Mark these cells.

nodes.label:
Cell where BOTH
are best responses?
nodes.note: A cell is a PSNE if and only if both players have marked it as a best response. Check each cell: does it carry marks for both players simultaneously?

nodes.label: That cell is a PSNE
nodes.note: Record this strategy profile as a Pure Strategy Nash Equilibrium. A game can have multiple PSNEs (e.g. coordination games) or none at all (e.g. Matching Pennies).

nodes.label:
More cells
to check?
nodes.note: Continue scanning remaining cells in the matrix to ensure all PSNEs are found, not just the first one encountered.

nodes.label: All PSNE found
nodes.note: All cells have been checked. The list of marked cells where both players are best-responding gives the complete set of PSNEs.

nodes.label:
No PSNE exists
Seek MSNE
nodes.note: No cell carries marks for both players. The game has no pure strategy Nash equilibrium. By Nash's theorem, at least one mixed strategy NE must exist.

charts.title: Computing Mixed Strategy Nash Equilibrium
charts.description: Indifference principle method for computing MSNE in a 2-player game.

nodes.label:
Start: 2-player game
with no or multiple PSNE
nodes.note: We apply the indifference principle to find a mixed strategy Nash equilibrium. This is appropriate when a game has no PSNE, or when we want to find additional NE beyond the pure ones.

nodes.label:
Parameterise P1 mix:
prob p on action A
nodes.note: Let Player 1 play action A with probability p and action B with probability 1-p. We use p as an unknown to be determined by making Player 2 indifferent.

nodes.label:
Make P2 indifferent:
set EU2(X) = EU2(Y), solve p
nodes.note: Player 2 will only mix if they are indifferent between their actions. Set Player 2's expected utility from action X equal to their expected utility from action Y, then solve for p.

nodes.label:
Parameterise P2 mix:
prob q on action X
nodes.note: Let Player 2 play action X with probability q and action Y with probability 1-q. We use q as an unknown to be determined by making Player 1 indifferent.

nodes.label:
Make P1 indifferent:
set EU1(A) = EU1(B), solve q
nodes.note: Player 1 will only mix if they are indifferent between their actions. Set Player 1's expected utility from action A equal to action B, then solve for q.

nodes.label:
Both p and q
in range [0,1]?
nodes.note: The values p and q must be valid probabilities. If both lie in [0,1] the solution is a genuine mixed NE. If either is outside [0,1], no fully mixed NE exists and we must reconsider the support.

nodes.label:
MSNE found:
(p,1-p) for P1, (q,1-q) for P2
nodes.note: The mixed strategy Nash equilibrium has Player 1 mixing with probabilities (p, 1-p) and Player 2 mixing with probabilities (q, 1-q). Both players are indifferent between their actions and have no incentive to deviate.

nodes.label:
Adjust support
or check boundary
nodes.note: The computed probabilities are not valid. Check whether a pure strategy NE on the boundary was missed, or consider a different support (subset of actions that each player mixes over).

charts.title: Maximin Strategy Procedure
charts.description: How to compute the maximin safety-level strategy for a player.

nodes.label:
Start: player i's
utility matrix
nodes.note: We want to find player i's maximin strategy — the strategy that maximises the worst-case payoff, assuming opponents play adversarially to minimise i's utility.

nodes.label:
For each pure strategy,
find minimum payoff
nodes.note: For each of player i's pure strategies, compute the minimum payoff across all opponent strategy combinations. This is the worst case if player i commits to that strategy.

nodes.label:
Select strategy with
highest minimum payoff
nodes.note: The pure maximin strategy is the one whose minimum payoff is highest. This is player i's best guarantee using only pure strategies.

nodes.label:
Can mixing improve
worst-case payoff?
nodes.note: Mixed strategies can sometimes guarantee a higher minimum payoff than any pure strategy alone. This occurs when mixing makes it harder for opponents to exploit a particular action. Check by setting worst-case payoffs equal across actions.

nodes.label:
Parameterise mix:
prob p on each action
nodes.note: Let player i mix over their actions with probability p on one action and (1-p) on another. The worst-case payoff under this mix depends on which opponent action minimises i's expected utility.

nodes.label:
Equate worst-case payoffs
across actions, solve for p
nodes.note: The optimal mixing probability equalises the minimum payoffs from each pure action. Set the expressions for worst-case utility under each pure opponent action equal to each other and solve for p.

nodes.label:
Mixed maximin
strategy found
nodes.note: The value of p that equalises worst-case payoffs gives player i a higher safety level than any pure strategy. This is the maximin strategy and the maximin value is the guaranteed expected payoff.

nodes.label:
Pure maximin
strategy is optimal
nodes.note: No mixed strategy can improve on the best pure strategy's worst-case payoff. The pure maximin strategy is optimal and the safety level equals the best pure minimum payoff.

charts.title: Solution Concepts: Strength and Containment
charts.description: Hierarchy of solution concepts showing containment and implication relationships.

nodes.label:
Normal Form Game
Solution Concepts
nodes.note: Multiple solution concepts exist for normal form games, varying in their strength (how much rationality they assume) and existence guarantees.

nodes.label:
Dominant Strategy
Equilibrium
nodes.note: The strongest solution concept. A dominant strategy is always optimal regardless of opponents' play. Requires no beliefs about others. Rare: exists in Prisoner's Dilemma but not most games.

nodes.label:
Strong
Equilibrium
nodes.note: No coalition of players (of any size) can jointly deviate to improve all coalition members' payoffs. Strictly stronger than Nash. Rarely exists.

nodes.label:
Pure Strategy
Nash Equilibrium
nodes.note: No individual player can unilaterally improve. May not exist (Matching Pennies) or may not be unique (Coordination Game). Every dominant strategy equilibrium is a PSNE.

nodes.label:
Mixed Strategy
Nash Equilibrium
nodes.note: Nash's 1950 theorem guarantees existence in every finite game. Every PSNE is a special case. Players randomise to keep opponents indifferent.

nodes.label:
Correlated
Equilibrium
nodes.note: A superset of Nash equilibria, allowing coordination via a shared random signal. Always exists and can be computed in polynomial time via linear programming.

nodes.label:
Rationalizable
Strategies
nodes.note: Survives iterated elimination of strategies that are never a best response. Every NE strategy is rationalizable, but rationalizable strategies may not be part of any NE.

nodes.label:
epsilon-Nash
Equilibrium
nodes.note: A relaxation of Nash equilibrium where no player can gain more than epsilon by deviating. Standard Nash is the special case where epsilon equals zero.
